title: Mammea subsessilifolia

This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and its relationship to other species within the family. The plant's classification is further supported by its morphological features, such as its leaves and flowers, which are typical of the Meliaceae family. As a result, Mammea subsessilifolia is classified as a member of the family Meliaceae within the genus Mammea.

intro: Mammea subsessilifolia, also known as the West Indian cherry or wild cherry, is a tropical plant species native to the Caribbean and Central America, characterized by its small, edible fruits and medicinal properties. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Mammea subsessilifolia include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. In traditional medicine, Mammea subsessilifolia is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, which can be used to treat various health conditions.
